audit_status.source_base: Greek Gospel text tradition, draft aligned to public-domain comparison layer later
audit_status.review_note: Compact complete draft reviewed for purity/tradition debate, Isaiah citation, Corban, heart-output teaching, Syrophoenician woman, and deaf man healing with Ephphatha.

verses.restored: The Pharisees and some of the scribes gathered to him, having come from Jerusalem.
They saw some of his disciples eating bread with common/unclean hands, that is, unwashed.
For the Pharisees and all the Judeans do not eat unless they wash their hands with the fist, holding the tradition of the elders.
When they come from the marketplace, they do not eat unless they wash; and there are many other things they received to hold: washings of cups, pitchers, and vessels.
The Pharisees and scribes asked him, Why do your disciples not walk according to the tradition of the elders, but eat bread with common/unclean hands?
He said to them, Isaiah prophesied beautifully about you hypocrites, as it has been written: This people honors me with lips, but their heart is far away from me.
In vain they worship me, teaching as teachings the commands of humans.
Leaving the command of the Divine, you hold the tradition of humans.
He said to them, Beautifully do you set aside the command of the Divine so that you may keep your tradition.
For Moses said, Honor your father and your mother, and, The one speaking evil of father or mother must surely come to an end.
But you say, If a person says to father or mother, Whatever you might have gained from me is Corban, that is, gift-offering,
then you no longer allow him to do anything for father or mother,
making void the word of the Divine by your tradition that you handed down; and many such things you do.
Calling the crowd again, he said to them, Hear me, all of you, and understand.
There is nothing outside a person that, entering into him, is able to make him common/unclean; but the things coming out of a person are what make the person common/unclean.
[Some later manuscripts add: If anyone has ears to hear, let him hear.]
When he entered a house away from the crowd, his disciples asked him about the parable.
He said to them, Are you also without understanding? Do you not perceive that everything outside entering a person is not able to make him common/unclean,
because it does not enter his heart but his belly, and goes out into the latrine, cleansing all foods.
He said, What comes out of a person, that makes the person common/unclean.
For from within, out of the heart of humans, come harmful reasonings, sexual immoralities, thefts, murders, adulteries,
greed, harmful acts, deceit, sensual excess, a harmful eye, slander, pride, foolishness.
All these harmful things come out from within and make a person common/unclean.
From there he rose and went away into the borders of Tyre. Entering a house, he wanted no one to know, yet he could not be hidden.
Immediately, a woman whose little daughter had an unclean spirit heard about him and came and fell at his feet.
The woman was Greek, Syrophoenician by birth. She asked him to cast the unclean spirit out of her daughter.
He said to her, Let the children be filled first, for it is not beautiful/good to take the children's bread and throw it to the little dogs.
She answered him, Lord, even the little dogs under the table eat from the children's crumbs.
He said to her, Because of this word, go; the unclean spirit has gone out from your daughter.
Going away to her house, she found the child lying on the bed and the unclean spirit gone out.
Again, going out from the borders of Tyre, he came through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee, in the middle of the Decapolis borders.
They brought to him a deaf man with difficulty speaking, and pleaded with him to place his hand on him.
Taking him away from the crowd privately, he put his fingers into his ears, and after spitting touched his tongue.
Looking up into heaven, he sighed deeply and said to him, Ephphatha, that is, Be opened.
His hearing was opened, the binding of his tongue was released, and he spoke plainly.
Jesus ordered them to tell no one; but the more he ordered them, the more abundantly they proclaimed it.
They were exceedingly astonished, saying, He has done all things beautifully/well; he even makes the deaf hear and the speechless speak.
